title: Variables & Quoting
summary: Variables in Bash store strings and are referenced with $. Quoting controls whether the shell expands variables and interprets special characters. Getting quoting wrong is one of the most common sources of bugs in shell scripts.
keyPoints: Assign without spaces: VAR=value (spaces around = cause errors)
Double quotes "" allow variable and command expansion
Single quotes '' treat everything literally — no expansion at all
Command substitution: VAR=$(command) stores the command's output
$1, $2, ...: positional arguments passed to the script
$#: number of arguments, $@: all arguments as separate words, $*: all as one string
Always double-quote variables: "$VAR" to prevent word splitting

exercises.prompt: Which quotes allow variable expansion inside a string?
exercises.explanation: Single quotes suppress all expansion. Double quotes allow $VAR and $(cmd) but prevent word splitting.

exercises.prompt: Which special variable holds all arguments passed to a script as a single string?
exercises.explanation: $* expands all positional parameters as a single word. $@ expands them as separate quoted words.

exercises.prompt: Complete the line to store the output of 'date' in a variable called TODAY.
exercises.explanation: $() runs the command in a subshell and substitutes its output. Backticks `` are an older equivalent.
